target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

target.title: Add plugin manifest contract for SecretRef provider integrations
target.body:
## Summary
- add a manifest-level SecretRef provider integration contract for plugins
- let plugin manifests declare exec provider presets that materialize existing `secrets.providers` config
- expose SDK types and cover manifest normalization/materialization with focused tests

## Why
External providers like Vault, OneCLI, 1Password, Bitwarden, and similar stores were already possible through manual exec SecretRef configuration. This makes them discoverable, portable, and installable as plugins without hardcoding provider-specific logic in OpenClaw core.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: External SecretRef providers could already be wired manually with `exec`, but plugin-owned Vault-style integrations were not discoverable or portable through plugin manifest metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local Podman setup with `quay.io/sallyom/openclaw:latest`, `hashicorp/vault:latest` in dev mode, external `claw-vault` plugin from https://github.com/sallyom/claw-vault, and OpenRouter model `openrouter/openai/gpt-4.1-nano`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started Vault dev mode, seeded `secret/providers/openrouter` from `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, started OpenClaw with `models.providers.openrouter.apiKey` set to `{ "source": "exec", "provider": "vault", "id": "providers/openrouter/apiKey" }`
- Additional live proof on the running openclaw: confirmed the git-installed `claw-vault` manifest declares `secretProviderIntegrations.vault` with `command: "${node}"` and `args: ["./vault-secret-ref-resolver.js"]`, confirmed model API keys are SecretRefs using provider `vault`, ran `openclaw secrets audit --allow-exec --json`, then ran `openclaw agent --local --agent somalley_larry --message "Reply with exactly: vault-secretref-larry-ok" --timeout 60000 --json`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Full terminal-command repro is in this Gist: https://gist.github.com/sallyom/50f4678b053eaa93c4311a5509e52aeb. Copied live output from `secrets reload`: `{"ok":true,"warningCount":0}`. Copied live output from the agent run included `vault-secretref-ok`. 
- Observed result after fix: OpenClaw reloaded SecretRefs with zero warnings in the original repro and completed live model turns using API keys resolved from Vault by the external `claw-vault` exec resolver. Also resolved two Vault-backed exec SecretRefs with no unresolved refs and completed a live OpenRouter agent turn through the Vault-resolved key.
- Users would use the external Vault plugin's `openclaw vault setup` helper to generate an OpenClaw secrets apply plan, for example `openclaw vault setup --openrouter-id providers/openrouter/apiKey --plan-out ./vault-secrets-plan.json`, then apply it with the generic secrets command. `openclaw vault setup` maps existing Vault secret ids into OpenClaw SecretRefs and provider config; it does not write secret values into Vault. `openclaw vault status --json` reports whether the Vault provider is configured.
- What was not tested: Production Vault deployment, non-dev Vault auth methods, 1Password/Bitwarden/OneCLI plugins.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this contract, the same Vault resolver could only be wired by hand through explicit `secrets.providers.vault` config; there was no plugin manifest preset for OpenClaw to discover/materialize.

## Verification
- `pnpm vitest run src/secrets/provider-integrations.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

target.title: fix(heartbeat): suppress fallback after route-matched message tool
target.body:
When heartbeat runs in message-tool-only mode, a successful generic message tool delivery can still be followed by duplicate free-text fallback narration in the same Telegram chat.

Fixes #84217

## Affected surface

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`
  - Adds route-matched message tool delivery evidence for heartbeat replies only after the generic message tool sent to the same provider/target/account route.
- `src/auto-reply/reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds internal reply-payload metadata for route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat-reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds a heartbeat-specific evidence reader.
- `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`
  - Suppresses free-text fallback only when the reply carries route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts`
  - Leaves heartbeat payloads available for heartbeat-specific route evidence instead of dropping them via generic message-tool dedupe first.
- Tests:
  - `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts`

## Scope

This is a parameter/evidence threading fix. It does not add a config flag, does not change heartbeat scheduling, and does not touch Slack identity, commitment, or channel-specific private paths.

The important semantic boundary is that "generic message tool delivery evidence" is not the same as "has outbound reply content". This PR only marks heartbeat replies when existing message-tool target evidence matches the same delivery route via the existing `resolveMessagingToolPayloadDedupe` route matcher. Global `messagingToolSentTexts` without route targets is intentionally not enough.

After Vincent's thread-route follow-up, I found one extra current-head issue while refreshing proof: generic reply-payload dedupe could drop the matched heartbeat payload before `heartbeat-runner` saw the route evidence. The current head keeps heartbeat payloads out of that generic dedupe layer so the heartbeat-specific suppression path receives the evidence and can still preserve no-route / wrong-topic fallback behavior.

## Prior art

Prior attempt #84273 was closed by contributor due to inability to provide live channel proof. This PR provides expanded source + runtime helper proof, with the helper semantic explicitly distinguishing "generic message tool delivery evidence" from "has outbound reply content" to address the message-delivery risk flagged in #84273.

The key difference from #84273 is route scope: #84273 marked heartbeat payloads after any message-tool send, while this PR only marks a heartbeat payload when committed `messagingToolSentTargets` match the reply route. If the message tool delivered to another route, or only global text evidence exists, heartbeat fallback still sends.

## Current-head maintenance proof

After Vincent's route/thread follow-up moved this PR to `f21343bfd4`, CI exposed one current-head regression: `checks-node-auto-reply-reply-agent-runner` timed out in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts` at `dedupes duplicate same-target reply text without suppressing unrelated finals`.

Root cause: route dedupe called `getChannelPlugin("telegram")` from the generic reply-payload path, which lazily loads the bundled Telegram plugin in this test/runtime path and can block the shard. Current head `df6f33925b` keeps the route/thread semantics from Vincent's follow-up, uses only the already-loaded channel registry for plugin-specific matching, and preserves internal route-string fallback for forms like `telegram:group:<chat>:topic:<thread>` without loading bundled channel implementations.

Current-head commands run locally:

```text
$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts -t "dedupes duplicate same-target reply text without suppressing unrelated finals"
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 46 skipped (47)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 26.25s

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  14 passed (14)
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  77 passed (77)
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 41.90s

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -t "heartbeat replies"
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  9 passed | 55 skipped (64)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 36.48s

$ pnpm check:changed --base origin/main
[check:changed] lanes=all
[check:changed] typecheck all
[check:changed] lint

Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
exit 0

$ git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
exit 0

$ gitleaks protect --staged --redact
INF 0 commits scanned.
INF no leaks found
```

Note: I did not rerun the earlier live Telegram helper on this current head. The current-head proof above is local source/runtime test coverage plus the prior PR body runtime helper proof retained below for the original route-suppression behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** heartbeat message-tool-only delivery should not send duplicate free-text fallback after the generic `message` tool has already delivered to the same route, but it must still send fallback when no same-route delivery evidence exists.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0, branch `wolf/heartbeat-suppress-after-message-tool`, head `14b8854108`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran a local OpenClaw runtime helper that imports production `runHeartbeatOnce`, seeds a real `openclaw.json`-shaped config and session store, registers a local Telegram outbound adapter, then executes three heartbeat ticks: same-route evidence, no-route evidence, and wrong-thread/topic-unmarked evidence.
- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the runtime helper:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=14b8854108 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr84975-proof/runtime-proof.mts
[runtime-helper-proof] repoHead=14b8854108
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "same-route-message-tool-evidence-suppresses-fallback",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": true,
  "telegramSendCount": 0,
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration that would duplicate same-route message-tool delivery."
}
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "no-route-evidence-keeps-fallback-visible",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": false,
  "telegramSendCount": 1,
  "sentTo": "<redacted-telegram-target>",
  "sentTextPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery without same-route evidence.",
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery without same-route evidence."
}
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "wrong-thread-or-topic-unmarked-evidence-keeps-fallback-visible",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": false,
  "telegramSendCount": 1,
  "sentTo": "<redacted-telegram-target>",
  "sentTextPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery for a different topic/thread.",
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery for a different topic/thread."
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** with route-scoped message-tool evidence, `runHeartbeatOnce` completes and does not invoke the Telegram send path (`telegramSendCount: 0`), so the fallback narration is suppressed. Without route evidence, and for the wrong-topic/wrong-thread-unmarked fallback case, the same runtime path invokes the Telegram send path once (`telegramSendCount: 1`), proving fallback remains available when the message tool did not prove same-route delivery.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live Telegram heartbeat tick with a real bot token/chat. I did not test Slack/WhatsApp live channels; the patch is heartbeat reply evidence and generic runner fallback suppression, with Telegram-shaped runtime coverage for the reported duplicate free-text path.

## Supplemental validation

### RED before fix

The new regression was first run before the implementation. `runHeartbeatOnce` still sent fallback text even when the reply carried route-scoped message-tool delivery evidence:

```text
$ pnpm test src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -t "message-tool delivery"

expected "spy" to not be called at all, but actually been called 1 times
received:

```

target.title: perf(plugins): cut plugin-system thrash from TUI cold start
target.body: Improvement to plugin loading performance on gateway and TUI cold start. BEFORE AFTER > Screenshots are from the pre-rebase trace. After rebasing past #85201 (Vincent's derived-snapshot freshness fix), the persisted-path wins below still apply in full; the on-disk number may be slightly smaller on workloads where cold start hits the derived path. Will refresh once I re-run a trace from a clean state. ## Summary Cuts duplicated plugin-system work during plugin resolve / cold start. Three classes of fix, ranked by impact: ### 1. Plumbing — `PluginMetadataSnapshot` threaded through resolve paths (~2–4s) `resolvePluginProviders`, `isPluginProvidersLoadInFlight`, `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider`, and `resolveExternalAuthProfilesWithPlugins` all dispatch to helpers that have a `manifestRegistry` fast path. They now thread the snapshot through so the fast path actually fires instead of every helper rebuilding the manifest registry from disk. `loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex` fires drop ~685 → ~10 per cold start. A second plumb threads `discovery?: PluginDiscoveryResult` through `applyPluginAutoEnable` → `detectPluginAutoEnableCandidates` → `resolvePluginAutoEnableReadiness` → `collectConfiguredChannelIds` → the bundled-channel leaf helpers, so the caller's already-resolved discovery short-circuits the cascade. This is the load-bearing perf fix and is independent of memoization — it works because we don't redundantly call `loadPluginMetadataSnapshot` within a single resolve chain. ### 2. Cache-busting — snapshot memo store/lookup keys now match (~7s on persisted path) Previously the memo computed its lookup key from the persisted index and stored entries under the derived index. Those indexes always differ in volatile fields (`generatedAtMs`, `refreshReason`), so the keys never agreed and every lookup missed. **Persisted-path hit rate goes from 24% → ~98%.** After #85201 landed on main, derived snapshots are intentionally excluded from the memo (so newly added plugins under derived discovery paths are discovered without restart). This PR's memo fix applies to the persisted/provided path, which is the steady-state hit path once the persisted registry has been written. ### 3. LRU capacity (supporting) Memo was single-slot and thrashed when two stable input shapes alternated (different `preferPersisted`, different `stateDir`, different config fingerprint). Capacity-8 LRU keeps the realistic set warm. Necessary precondition for the cache-busting fix to land cleanly — without LRU, the memo would still self-evict across distinct memoKeys. Additionally `findFastPathRegistryState` shares the persisted-registry-state computation across entries so a cold memoKey can piggy-back on a sibling entry's fast-path result. ## Setup-CLI-backend owner regression fix [`bd58a706`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/bd58a706) — the rewritten single owner-scan in `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider` was missing `plugin.setup?.cliBackends`. Pre-PR the no-registry fallback went through `resolvePluginContributionOwners({ contribution: "cliBackends" })` which includes both top-level and setup CLI backends; the replacement scan needed the same coverage. Includes regression test in `providers.test.ts`. ## Verification - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit --project tsconfig.core.json` — clean - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts src/plugins/providers.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/load-context.test.ts` — pass - `pnpm build && pnpm openclaw tui` from a cold process (pre-rebase trace) — freeze 32s → 13–16s, snapshot memo hit rate 24% → ~98% on persisted path, per-plugin `package.json` reads ~914 → ~370 Not run: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm check`, sibling-process plugin-install interleaving. Post-rebase TUI trace pending. ## Coexistence with #85201 #85201 (Vincent Koc, merged today) added `registrySource` to the snapshot and gates `canMemoizePluginMetadataSnapshotResult` on `registrySource !== "derived"` — derived snapshots stay out of the process memo and the runtime current-snapshot handoff. This PR coexists with that boundary: the plumbing/discovery work is unaffected, the LRU + symmetric-key + fast-path-sharing changes apply to persisted/provided memo entries only. ## Follow-on PR An `installRecords` threading optimization that cut ~264 sync disk reads (~140ms freeze) was extracted into a separate PR ([#85026](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85026), already merged).

target.title: fix(media): avoid eager provider startup for audio listing
target.body:
## Summary
- list audio transcription providers from manifest metadata instead of constructing the runtime media-understanding registry
- add missing bundled SenseAudio provider metadata so `capability audio providers` can report it without loading runtime code
- apply control-plane plugin availability filtering to media-understanding manifest metadata, including global `plugins.enabled: false`
- narrow plugin capability provider loading to config-requested media providers when no active registry exists

## Why
`openclaw capability audio providers` only needs provider metadata for status/listing output. Building the runtime registry can eagerly load provider implementations and plugin code, which is slower and can trigger work unrelated to listing configured audio providers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw capability audio providers --json` should list manifest-declared audio providers without eager runtime startup, include bundled SenseAudio metadata, and omit bundled plugin providers when plugins are globally disabled.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node.js v24.2.0, built CLI entrypoint from this PR branch after patching.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
CI=true OPENCLAW_BUILD_VERBOSE=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_TSDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d) HOME=$(mktemp -d) node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json
tmp_home=$(mktemp -d); tmp_state=$(mktemp -d); cfg=$(mktemp); printf '%s\n' '{"plugins":{"enabled":false}}' > "$cfg"; OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmp_state" HOME="$tmp_home" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$cfg" node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live terminal output from the built CLI:

```json
{
  "id": "senseaudio",
  "capabilities": ["audio"],
  "defaultModels": {
    "audio": "senseaudio-asr-pro-1.5-260319"
  },
  "available": true,
  "configured": false,
  "selected": false
}
```

Disabled-plugin config copied live output:

```json
[]
```

- Observed result after fix: The real CLI provider list includes `senseaudio` with the expected audio default model, and the same command returns an empty provider list when run with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` pointing to a config containing `{"plugins":{"enabled":false}}`. The focused test shard also passed: media-understanding 10 tests and cli capability 76 tests.
- What was not tested: I did not call the SenseAudio service or verify a live transcription; this PR only changes provider discovery/listing metadata.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Review report showed SenseAudio missing from `capability audio providers` output and bundled providers still appearing when plugins were globally disabled.

## Root Cause
The CLI listing path moved to manifest metadata, but SenseAudio did not declare media-understanding provider metadata in its bundled manifest. The metadata builder also delegated availability filtering without first honoring the global plugin disable switch, so disabled plugin providers could still be listed.

## Tests
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_BUILD_VERBOSE=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_TSDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`
- `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d) HOME=$(mktemp -d) node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json`
- disabled-plugin config CLI proof command above
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/senseaudio/openclaw.plugin.json src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Hygiene
- committed with GitHub noreply author email
- staged only the four intended source/test files
- checked the staged diff for obvious credential patterns before committing

## Summary
- add saved-report comparison mode to `scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts`

## Why
The follow-up performance PRs need repeatable before/after numbers. The benchmark harness could already save JSON reports, but comparing two saved reports required ad-hoc one-off scripts. This makes the comparison reusable for future PR bodies and review proof.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: saved CLI startup benchmark reports can now be compared directly, with text output for PR proof and JSON output for downstream tooling.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node.js v24.2.0, using saved benchmark JSON reports produced from the #85396 root-help performance test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --compare-baseline /tmp/root-help-before.json --compare-candidate /tmp/root-help-after.json
node scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --compare-baseline /tmp/root-help-before.json --compare-candidate /tmp/root-help-after.json --json
```

target.title: fix(codex): unsubscribe bound threads after turns
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #85458.
- Adds best-effort `thread/unsubscribe` cleanup after Codex app-server bound turns finish or fail.
- Covers missing-thread recovery when the replacement thread retry fails, preserving the saved replacement binding while releasing app-server subscriptions.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt-thread-cleanup.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex app-server conversation bindings now best-effort unsubscribe the bound thread after every turn attempt, including the missing-thread recovery retry path, so a recreated bound thread is not left subscribed when the retry rejects.
Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout on the PR branch, using the real Codex app-server conversation-binding runtime entrypoint with an isolated temporary HOME/CODEX_HOME and a stdio app-server process that records JSON-RPC calls.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx .tmp-codex-proof.ts`.
Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the runtime command:

```json
{
  "result": {
    "handled": true,
    "reply": {
      "text": "Codex app-server turn failed: retry failed after recovery"
    }
  },
  "calls": [
    { "id": 2, "method": "turn/start", "threadId": "thread-old" },
    { "id": 3, "method": "thread/unsubscribe", "threadId": "thread-old" },
    { "id": 4, "method": "thread/start" },
    { "id": 5, "method": "turn/start", "threadId": "thread-new" },
    { "id": 6, "method": "thread/unsubscribe", "threadId": "thread-new" }
  ],
  "savedThreadId": "thread-new"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The command exited 0 and returned `Codex app-server turn failed: retry failed after recovery`, with both old and replacement thread unsubscribe calls recorded in order.
What was not tested: No live Codex hosted session was used; the proof isolates auth and exercises the OpenClaw runtime entrypoint against a local stdio app-server process, plus the targeted Vitest coverage listed above.

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. Fast bail-outs make the cost a single `readdirSync` for users without legacy sidecars. Declining writes a 24h cool-off marker in the state dir (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) so we never pester the user every command. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the prompt — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation). Architecturally cleaner than threading TTY-awareness into the embedded runtime: the trigger reuses doctor's existing migrate function as-is, and "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" stays one decision, one place.

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` → `OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker.

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule, plus end-to-end migrate / decline / cool-off using `createOpenClawTestState`.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the new headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted-boundary.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` — 61/61 green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.
- Wider `src/agents/auth-profiles` shard has the same 5 file / 26 test pre-existing flakes vs `origin/main` (`--isolate=false` cross-file mock leakage in unrelated OAuth tests). My new tests do not contribute to those failures; if anything, isolating mine reduces the count.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

target.title: fix(microsoft-foundry): filter Anthropic deployments during onboard
target.body:
## Summary

The built-in `microsoft-foundry` provider only supports OpenAI-compatible Foundry APIs (`chat/completions`, `responses`). However, Anthropic (Claude) deployments on Azure AI Foundry use a different API shape (`/anthropic/v1/messages`) that is not wired yet. This causes silent 404 failures at runtime after apparently successful onboarding — the user selects a Claude deployment, configuration looks correct, but requests fail.

## Changes

- Add `isAnthropicFoundryDeployment()` to detect Claude model names
- Filter Anthropic deployments from the selection list during onboard
- Show an informative note with workaround (custom provider with `anthropic-messages` API) when Anthropic deployments are skipped
- Provide a specific error message when *only* Anthropic deployments exist in the resource
- Add tests for the detection function (11 test cases)

## Why Option B (guardrail)

The issue suggests two approaches: full Anthropic API support (Option A) or a guardrail (Option B). This PR implements Option B because:
- It prevents the confusing "config succeeded but runtime fails" experience immediately
- The workaround (custom provider) is clearly communicated to users
- Full Anthropic Foundry support can be added later without breaking this guardrail

## Testing

- 46 tests pass (`pnpm test extensions/microsoft-foundry/index.test.ts`)
- New tests cover: Claude model name detection (4 variants), non-Anthropic rejection (7 variants including edge cases), and a regression where `modelName` is missing and the deployment `name` carries the Claude prefix

## Real behavior proof

Live Azure Foundry redacted output isn't available to me, so the proof here is a stash-toggle comparison against the actual Azure list-deployments shape from #60546 (deployments where `properties.model.name` is omitted on non-OpenAI entries). Probe `scripts/repro-60546.mjs` was not committed.

PRE-fix (modelName-only classifier — current `main` behavior):
```
PRE-fix  supported (persisted + offered): [ 'claude-opus-4-6', 'claude-sonnet-4-6', 'gpt-5.4-prod' ]
PRE-fix  anthropic (filtered):            []
```

POST-fix (this PR — falls back to `deployment.name` via `resolveConfiguredModelNameHint`):
```
POST-fix supported (persisted + offered): [ 'gpt-5.4-prod' ]
POST-fix anthropic (filtered):            [ 'claude-opus-4-6', 'claude-sonnet-4-6' ]
```

Without the fix the two `claude-*` deployments are persisted and offered, then fail at runtime via `/openai/v1`. With the fix they're filtered out and surfaced via the unsupported-deployments note.

A maintainer override can be recorded here if redacted live evidence is required.

Fixes #60546

target.title: fix(auto-reply): surface progress for long reply runs
target.body:
## Summary

- add a deterministic reply-run progress watchdog for long user-triggered runs
- reset the watchdog whenever a visible reply/progress callback fires
- add an empty-final fallback so user-triggered runs that complete without visible output surface a clear error instead of going silent

## Why

Today stuck-session diagnostics can detect long processing runs, but they are log-only. If a user-triggered reply run keeps working, stalls, or completes with no final payload, the user can see silence even though the gateway has state about the active run. This patch makes that failure mode visible without relying on model-generated filler.

## Behavior

- Non-heartbeat, non-silent reply runs with an outbound block-reply route send a deterministic progress notice after 5 minutes of no visible activity, then at most every 5 minutes thereafter.
- Any visible partial/block/tool-result callback resets the quiet timer.
- Terminal reply-run phases stop the watchdog.
- If a user-triggered run completes with no final payload and no other explicit visible delivery path handled it, OpenClaw returns a deterministic error payload.

## Validation

- `pnpm check:changed`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-watchdog.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/empty-final-reply.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

Note: I also ran `pnpm test:changed`; it hit an unrelated existing `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts` assertion (`__exit__:78` vs `__exit__:1`). The changed-area checks and focused tests above pass.

target.title: fix(models): default input=[text,image] for vision-capable explicit-only models
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #71921.

When a user's `openclaw.json` contains a static model catalog for a provider (e.g. `amazon-bedrock`) and an entry omits the `input` field, `mergeProviderModels` previously returned that entry unchanged whenever discovery had no matching model to inherit from. Downstream `modelSupportsImages` (`dist/images-CzeTciMv.js`) reads `model.input?.includes("image") ?? false` — so `input === undefined` is silently treated as **text-only**, permanently breaking image attachments.

The full chain that produces this state in the wild:

1. `extensions/amazon-bedrock/discovery.ts` imports from `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock`. That package is declared in the extension's `package.json` with `stageRuntimeDependencies: true` and is staged into `~/.openclaw/plugin-runtime-deps/.../node_modules/` on first boot after install/upgrade.
2. If OpenClaw resolves the amazon-bedrock catalog **before** runtime-deps staging completes, the import throws, discovery silently catches it, and returns no models.
3. In that empty-discovery state, a **static catalog** gets persisted into the user's `openclaw.json` for `models.providers.amazon-bedrock.models` — entries carrying `id`, `name`, `contextWindow`, `maxTokens`, but no `input`.
4. On subsequent boots, even after `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` is installed and discovery works correctly, the user still carries that frozen static catalog. `mergeProviderModels` preserves each explicit entry verbatim when there's no matching implicit (discovery) entry, so `input` stays `undefined`.
5. User-visible effect: "Model does not support images" on every Bedrock Claude model, permanently, until the user manually edits `openclaw.json` or `~/.openclaw/agents/*/agent/models.json` to add `input: ["text", "image"]`.

## The fix

Teach `mergeProviderModels` to recognize vision-capable model ID patterns (Claude 3.5+/4.x, GPT-4o / 4.1 / 5 / o-series, Gemini 1.5+, Llama 3.2+ / 4.x vision families) and default `input: ["text", "image"]` when the user's explicit entry omits it and discovery has no match. Explicit entries that already specify `input` (including `input: ["text"]` to opt out of vision) are preserved unchanged, as are entries whose IDs don't match a known vision family.

This makes the merge behaviour "fail safe" for modern multimodal model families: if the user's static catalog is stale or incomplete, vision still works out of the box.

## What this does **not** attempt to fix

Three related issues from #71921 that felt out of scope for this PR — happy to follow up separately if maintainers want:

1. **Bundle `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` directly** instead of staging via runtime-deps, to eliminate the first-boot race that seeds the stale static catalog in the first place.
2. **Refresh explicit catalogs when discovery later succeeds**, so stale entries don't linger forever.
3. **Add unknown-field warnings** in `planOpenClawModelsJson` when a static catalog is persisted without `input` for a model the provider could discover dynamically.

## Tests

New file: `src/agents/models-config.merge.explicit-input-default.test.ts`

- ✅ Defaults Claude Opus 4.x explicit-only entries to `[text, image]`
- ✅ Defaults Sonnet 4.x and Haiku 4.x explicit-only entries
- ✅ Defaults GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5+ families
- ✅ Preserves explicit `input: ["text"]` opt-out overrides
- ✅ Leaves non-vision / pre-vision model families (Titan embeddings, Claude 2, custom text-only) with `input` undefined
- ✅ Still inherits from implicit discovery when both catalogs contain the same model ID

All 6 tests pass locally. Existing `model-catalog.test.ts` and `models-config.*.test.ts` pass without regression.

## Reproduction / user workaround

Workaround for users hitting this before the fix ships:

```bash
python3 - << 'PY'
import json, pathlib
for p in pathlib.Path.home().glob('.openclaw/agents/*/agent/models.json'):
    d = json.loads(p.read_text())
    for m in d.get('providers',{}).get('amazon-bedrock',{}).get('models', []):
        mid = m.get('id','').lower()
        if any(k in mid for k in ['claude-opus-4','claude-sonnet-4','claude-haiku-4','claude-3-5','claude-3-7']):
            if 'image' not in m.get('input', []):
                m['input'] = ['text','image']
    p.write_text(json.dumps(d, indent=2))

p = pathlib.Path.home() / '.openclaw' / 'openclaw.json'
d = json.loads(p.read_text())
for m in d.get('models',{}).get('providers',{}).get('amazon-bedrock',{}).get('models', []):
    mid = m.get('id','').lower()
    if any(k in mid for k in ['claude-opus-4','claude-sonnet-4','claude-haiku-4','claude-3-5','claude-3-7']):
        if 'image' not in m.get('input', []):
            m['input'] = ['text','image']
p.write_text(json.dumps(d, indent=2))
PY
openclaw gateway restart
```

Tested end-to-end on OpenClaw 2026.4.24 with Claude Opus 4.7 on us-east-1 Bedrock.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bedrock vision-capable models (Claude 3.5+/4.x, etc.) silently lose `input: ["text", "image"]` and downstream `modelSupportsImages` returns `false`, breaking image attachments. Bug from #71921. Reproduces whenever `mergeProviderModels` sees an explicit (static-catalog) entry without `input` and no matching implicit (discovery) entry to inherit from — exactly the state seeded after a first-boot race against `@aws-sdk/client-bedrock` runtime-deps staging.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 arm64 Mac mini, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 (commit 50a2481), Node 25.8.0, Claude Opus 4.7 via Amazon Bedrock `us-east-1` (model id `us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7`). Author's actual production OpenClaw install, not a sandbox.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1 — inspect the merged catalog state on the live install:

```bash
python3 -c "import json,pathlib;d=json.loads((pathlib.Path.home()/'.openclaw'/'openclaw.json').read_text());[print(m['id'],'input=',m.get('input','<<UNDEFINED>>')) for m in d['models']['providers']['amazon-bedrock']['models']]"
```

Step 2 — apply the merged regex from `src/agents/models-config.merge.ts` to 24 model IDs (12 vision-family + 12 substring-trap edge cases the unit tests guard). Full script linked under After-fix evidence.

Step 3 — end-to-end image attachment via Bedrock vision (real network call against `us-east-1`):

```bash
aws bedrock-runtime invoke-model \
  --model-id us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7 \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --body fileb://payload.json \
  --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out \
  /tmp/out.json
```

`payload.json` contains the multimodal message body (image + text content blocks).

**After-fix evidence:**

Live screenshot rendered and analyzed by Bedrock Claude Opus 4.7 in the same conversation:

![Real behavior proof — OpenClaw 2026.5.18, Bedrock Opus 4.7](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/03-real-behavior-proof.png)

Linked terminal artifacts:
- Environment dump: [01-environment.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/01-environment.txt)
- 24-case merge-regex verification (with output): [02-merge-logic-proof.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/02-merge-logic-proof.txt)
- Live `aws bedrock-runtime invoke-model` transcript with token counts: [04-bedrock-vision-live.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaronedell/openclaw/64725aa8f591f1564eea044f0c7027d2bc43e8a9/.pr-proofs/72404/04-bedrock-vision-live.txt)

Live Bedrock Opus 4.7 response (verbatim, from artifact 04):

```
=== Bedrock Claude Opus 4.7 response ===
**H1 title:**
OpenClaw PR #72404 — Real behavior proof

**Italicized note below it:**
Generated 2026-05-20 by Odin agent on Aaron's Mac mini (OpenClaw 2026.5.18, Claude Opus 4.7 via Bedrock us-east-1)

**Green check marks (✅) in the verification table:** 12

stop_reason: end_turn
input_tokens: 2228
output_tokens: 130
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 9 Bedrock Claude vision-family entries in the live `openclaw.json` carry `input=['text','image']` after the merge. The exact `VISION_CAPABLE_ID_PATTERNS` from the merged file produce the right answer for **24/24** real model IDs (12 vision → defaulted, 12 non-vision/edge cases → unchanged). End-to-end image attachment to `us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` via `bedrock-runtime invoke-model` succeeds on the first call: model reads the PNG, quotes the H1 and italicized timestamp verbatim, counts the 12 ✅ checkmarks correctly, exits with `stop_reason: end_turn`, 2228 input tokens / 130 output tokens. Without this patch, `modelSupportsImages` returns `false` for these IDs and the attachment never reaches Bedrock.

**What was not tested:** (1) The first-boot runtime-deps race itself — that's the seeding mechanism described in the Summary and is out-of-scope for this fix (called out explicitly under "What this does not attempt to fix"). (2) Non-Bedrock providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Llama vision); their regex coverage is verified by the new unit tests but no live multimodal call was issued against them in this proof. (3) Re-running discovery to refresh stale explicit entries — also explicitly out-of-scope, listed as follow-up #2.

target.title: fix: /allowlist --store bypasses channel configWrites policy

- Problem: An authorized sender on a channel where `configWrites=false` can invoke `/allowlist add|remove --store <id>` to add or remove entries from the persistent pairing allowlist store, defeating the operator's intent to block channel-initiated access-control writes. Because pairing-store entries are merged into the effective DM `allowFrom` list at authorization time, this bypass grants or revokes DM access for future senders without the operator's consent.

target.title: chore(ci): harden package-manager resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: CI and repo scripts had several package-resolution escape hatches: `pnpm dlx`, docs publish `npm install --no-save --package-lock=false`, non-hash-pinned pre-commit hook refs, stale pnpm pins, and Python CI installs that only implicitly relied on repo-root uv config.
- Why it matters: These paths are package supply-chain risk multipliers. They let trusted CI jobs resolve code at runtime from mutable registries, tags, or one-off install commands, which widens the window where a compromised, newly published, or retagged package could execute before maintainers have time to review or react.
- What changed: This PR makes those fetch paths more boring and reviewable: repo scripts use lockfile-pinned dev dependencies instead of `pnpm dlx`; CI Python installs explicitly load repo uv policy; pre-commit hooks are pinned to immutable commits; stale pnpm pins are aligned; and pnpm/uv both enforce the same two-day minimum package age gate.
- Regression guardrail: Root `AGENTS.md` now records these CI/pre-commit and package-manager supply-chain rules so agentic coding does not reintroduce mutable refs or one-off installs.
- Lockfile note: The `pnpm-lock.yaml` diff is expected. Replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` means the tools previously fetched on demand now live as dev dependencies, so their transitive dependency graphs are reviewed and pinned in the lockfile. Most of the noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: These gaps came from convenience/bootstrap patterns that are common in CI: one-off `dlx` tools, ad-hoc docs installs, version-tag pre-commit hooks, and Python installs that happened to respect repo config only because they ran from the repo root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no single guardrail preventing package-manager commands from resolving fresh packages outside the repo lockfile or configured age gate.
- Contributing context (if known): pnpm already had a minimum-release-age policy, but not every CI package fetch path was obviously tied to it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR reduces package-fetch risk by making CI installs prefer lockfile/configured package-manager paths, immutable hook refs, and uv's repo-scoped `exclude-newer = "2 days"` policy. The lockfile changes are intentional: replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` requires adding those tools as dev dependencies so their transitive graphs are reviewed and pinned. Most of the lockfile noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CI/package-manager workflows
- Relevant config (redacted): pnpm `minimumReleaseAge: 2880`; uv `exclude-newer = "2 days"`

### Steps

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/ci.yml pnpm-workspace.yaml pyproject.toml`
4. `pnpm check:changed`

### Expected

- Install and build pass.
- Changed-file formatting passes.
- Package-manager hardening does not introduce build failures.

### Actual

- Install passed.
- Build passed.
- Changed-file formatting passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, typecheck, lint, and import-cycle checks; full tests still fail in existing gateway/auth expectation cases unrelated to these package-manager changes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Relevant local check summary:

```text
[check:changed] summary
   1.19s  ok         conflict markers
  19.42s  ok         typecheck all
```

target.title: fix(agents): scope provider discovery from configured models
target.body:
## Summary

Reduces unnecessary provider/plugin discovery work by scoping non-live provider discovery to the plugins implied by configured models and providers.

While profiling slow OpenClaw startup, the hot path was broad plugin manifest/provider discovery even when the active config only referenced a specific model provider. This change narrows discovery when config contains model/provider hints, while preserving broad discovery when no hints exist.

## Changes

- Collect configured model references from agent defaults, fallbacks, subagent models, and named model entries.
- Resolve owning plugin ids from configured providers and model refs.
- Use that plugin-id filter for non-live provider discovery when hints are present.
- Preserve broad non-live discovery when no model/provider hints are configured.
- Add regression coverage for both scoped and broad fallback behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/models-config.providers.live-filter.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

## Notes

AI-assisted contribution. I profiled the local CPU hotspot, verified it came from provider/plugin discovery, and tested the scoped discovery behavior locally.

Behavior addressed: Fresh bare `openclaw` now starts classic onboarding instead of Crestodian when the active config is missing, empty, or metadata-only; configured installs still open Crestodian, and invalid configs still avoid onboarding so repair remains reachable.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout at `dae18659d42dcf2613990ca4895070bb94f63b1b`, Node `v24.14.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, isolated temp OpenClaw state/config, interactive terminal driven by `expect`.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output included `┌  OpenClaw setup`; `src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts` now covers missing config, empty config, metadata-only config, non-TTY fresh automation guidance, and configured Crestodian routing.

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.

target.title: feat(irc): support markdown messages via draft/multiline
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for sending markdown-formatted messages over IRC using the `draft/multiline` CAP extension. This preserves newlines in bot responses, allowing IRC clients to properly render markdown content.

## Motivation

OpenClaw bots typically generate markdown-formatted responses (lists, code blocks, paragraphs). Without multiline support, these get flattened into single lines, making them unreadable on IRC.

The `draft/multiline` spec (https://ircv3.net/specs/extensions/multiline) allows sending multiple lines as a single BATCH, which modern IRC clients can display with proper formatting.

## Changes

- **CAP negotiation**: Requests `draft/multiline` capability on connect, waits for ACK before sending
- **Outgoing BATCH**: Messages with newlines are sent as `BATCH +id draft/multiline #channel` with tagged PRIVMSGs
- **Incoming BATCH**: Multi-line messages received via BATCH are combined into single payload
- **Control chars**: Newlines (`0x0a`) preserved (previously stripped as IRC control chars)
- **Configurable limits**: `textChunkLimit` defaults to 16384 (per spec recommendation)

## Compatibility

- **Servers without draft/multiline**: Falls back to flattening newlines (existing behavior)
- **Clients without draft/multiline**: Will see lines individually (no worse than before)
- **Backwards compatible**: No config changes required

## Testing

Tested on Ergo IRC server with `draft/multiline` support. Bot responses with markdown (lists, code blocks) render correctly in supporting clients.

target.title: fix(webchat): slash menu clipped by overflow and missing keyboard scroll
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The webchat slash command menu (`/`) is completely invisible — typing `/` changes the input border color and arrow-key selection works, but the dropdown never appears. This is because `.agent-chat__input` uses `overflow: hidden` (for textarea scroll containment), which clips the absolutely-positioned slash menu that renders above the input via `position: absolute; bottom: 100%`.
- Additionally, when the slash menu has more items than the visible scroll area, keyboard navigation (ArrowUp/ArrowDown) does not auto-scroll the active item into view — users must manually scroll with the mouse.
- What changed: Introduced `.agent-chat__input-wrap` as a positioning parent for the slash menu, so it renders outside the `overflow: hidden` container. Added `scrollIntoView({ block: "nearest" })` after each arrow-key index change.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No modifications to slash command logic, command execution, or any non-UI code.

![slash-menu-fix](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9623f14a-9a7d-43a8-82fe-f75d5be85ba2)
- Here above demonstrates the fix.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Slash command menu now appears above the input box when typing `/` in webchat.
- Arrow-key navigation in the slash menu auto-scrolls to keep the active item visible.
- No changes to existing keyboard shortcuts (Tab to fill, Enter to select, Escape to close).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 25.2.0)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22+
- Integration/channel: Webchat (Control UI)

### Steps

1. Start gateway: `openclaw gateway run --bind loopback --port 18789 --force`
2. Open `http://127.0.0.1:18789/webchat/`
3. Type `/` in the chat input

### Expected

- Slash command menu appears above the input box
- ArrowDown/ArrowUp navigates through commands with auto-scroll

### Actual (before fix)

- Input border changes color (`:focus-within` fires), arrow keys + Enter work (DOM exists), but the menu is completely invisible — clipped by `overflow: hidden`
- When menu is made visible via other means, keyboard navigation past visible items requires manual mouse scrolling

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording

Before fix — typing `/` shows no menu (only border color change):
The DOM is present and functional (keyboard selection works), but the popup is clipped to zero height by `overflow: hidden` on the parent.

After fix — slash menu visible and keyboard-scrollable:
Menu appears above input; ArrowDown past visible items auto-scrolls the list.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Built UI locally via `pnpm ui:build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway run`
  - Confirmed slash menu appears when typing `/`
  - Confirmed ArrowDown/ArrowUp auto-scrolls active item into view
  - Confirmed Tab fills command, Enter selects, Escape closes
  - Confirmed input box border-radius, textarea scroll, and backdrop-filter unaffected
  - Confirmed mobile layout selector updated (`.agent-chat__input-wrap` in `layout.mobile.css`)
- What I did **not** verify:
  - macOS/iOS native app webchat (only tested browser webchat)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.agent-chat__input-wrap` wrapper — move `margin`/`flex-shrink` back to `.agent-chat__input` and remove the wrapper `<div>` in `chat.ts`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If a CSS rule targets `.agent-chat__input` for margin/positioning, it may need updating to `.agent-chat__input-wrap`.

## Files changed

- `ui/src/styles/chat/layout.css` — Added `.agent-chat__input-wrap` class; moved `margin` and `flex-shrink` from `.agent-chat__input` to the wrapper
- `ui/src/styles/layout.mobile.css` — Updated mobile override selector from `.agent-chat__input` to `.agent-chat__input-wrap`
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` — Wrapped input bar in `.agent-chat__input-wrap`; moved `renderSlashMenu` outside `.agent-chat__input`; added `scrollSlashMenuActiveIntoView()` helper called after ArrowUp/ArrowDown navigation

**AI-assisted:** Built with Claude Code. Tested manually on local gateway webchat.
